insights.recommendation: Reallocate budget towards Referral and Recruiter Outreach channels while reducing spend on LinkedIn.

evidence.key_insight: Referral has the highest offer rate (41.2%) and lowest dropout, while LinkedIn has the lowest offer rate (12.5%), indicating inefficient spending.

alternative.trade_off: Using a more general approach may yield slower optimization at a potentially higher cost.

Hackathon has a strong 32.3% offer rate but only 40.0% acceptance, suggesting strong initial fit but weak closing. LinkedIn has the worst offer rate at 12.5% with 50% acceptance — high screening cost, low yield.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"sourcing_quality\",\"category\":\"channel_performance\"},\"id\":\"src-001\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Sourcing effectiveness by position type. evaluation.reasoning: The recommendation is well-grounded, actionable, and aligns perfectly with the golden dataset expectations for the referral channel.

insights.recommendation: Add a salary range to job descriptions to reduce compensation mismatch rejections.

evidence.key_insight: The compensation mismatch rate of 13.2% is well above the industry benchmark of 5-10%, pointing to potential misalignment in salary expectations.

alternative.trade_off: This alternative might offer faster processing but with lower accuracy in recognizing nuanced patterns.

insights.rag_context: [{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Industry benchmarks for rejection patterns in technical hiring. Typical rejection rates: Engineering roles 55-65%, Data Science 50-60%, Product 45-55%. Expected early-stage (R1) rejection rate: 35-45% — screens out unqualified applicants. Expected compensation mismatch rate: 5-10% — above 12% signals JD salary range not being shared. Background check failure rate benchmark: 2-4%; above 8% suggests insufficient pre-screening. Culture fit rejection rate: 5-10%; above 12% may signal inconsistent culture definition across panels. No-show benchmark: under 5%; above 7% suggests poor candidate engagement or scheduling friction.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"rejection_pattern\",\"category\":\"industry_rejection_benchmarks\"},\"id\":\"rej-004\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Rejection distribution by interview round (stage analysis). Round 1: 23 rejections (20.2%) — high early-stage dropout suggests JD-to-expectation mismatch. Round 2: 31 rejections (27.2%) — largest dropout stage, likely technical screen. Round 3: 21 rejections (18.4%). Round 4: 23 rejections (20.2%). Round 5: 9 rejections (7.9%). Round 6: 7 rejections (6.1%). Benchmark: healthy funnels drop 30-40% at Round 1, 20-25% at Round 2. High Round 4 rejections (20%) relative to Round 3 may indicate panel quality issues late in process.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"rejection_pattern\",\"category\":\"rejection_by_stage\"},\"id\":\"rej-002\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Rejection reason breakdown (114 rejections from 200 candidates, 57% rejection rate). Top reasons: Role cancelled (15, 13.2%), Compensation mismatch (15, 13.2%), Underqualified (13, 11.4%), Communication issues (12, 10.5%), Technical skills (11, 9.6%), Background check failed (11, 9.6%), Culture fit (10, 8.8%), Overqualified (10, 8.8%), No show (9, 7.9%), Better candidate selected (8, 7.0%). Role cancellations and compensation mismatches together account for 26.3% — these are process failures, not candidate failures.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"rejection_pattern\",\"category\":\"rejection_reasons_overall\"},\"id\":\"rej-001\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Recommended actions to reduce avoidable rejections. (1) Add salary range to JD upfront — eliminates most compensation mismatch rejections. (2) Improve Round 1 screening criteria — 20% rejection at R1 suggests JD clarity issue. (3) Implement interviewer calibration sessions — communication and culture fit vary by panel. (4) Add a 24-hour reminder for scheduled interviews — reduces no-shows. (5) Review role cancellation process — 15 cancelled roles wastes candidate pipeline and brand trust. (6) Add an overqualified/underqualified pre-screen question at application stage.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"rejection_pattern\",\"category\":\"rejection_improvement_actions\"},\"id\":\"rej-005\"}"}]

evaluation.reasoning: The insight correctly identifies compensation mismatch as a key issue and aligns well with the golden dataset's expectations.

insights.recommendation: Rebalance 12-15 panels from Sam Rivera to Casey Kim and Morgan Chen immediately.

evidence.key_insight: Sam Rivera's panel load is significantly above the safe maximum, risking burnout and inconsistent scoring.

alternative.trade_off: More detailed analysis may take longer to yield insights.

insights.rag_context: [{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Panel rebalancing strategy for overloaded interviewers. To rebalance: move 12-15 panels from Sam Rivera to Casey Kim and Morgan Chen (capacity available). Rotate Quinn Diaz (57) and Jordan Lee (56) into senior-level panels they haven't covered yet. Establish a panel rotation policy: no interviewer should be on more than 3 panels per week. Add a scheduling rule in ATS: flag any interviewer exceeding 70% of average panel load. Cross-train interviewers across role types to improve rebalancing flexibility.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"panel_load_balancer\",\"category\":\"rebalancing_strategy\"},\"id\":\"panel-003\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Interviewer overload risk assessment. Industry benchmark: healthy interviewer load is 4-6 interviews per week, or 15-25 per month. Over a hiring cycle, 40-50 total panels is considered sustainable without calibration fatigue. Sam Rivera at 77 panels is 54-93% above safe maximum — high risk of scoring inconsistency. Jamie Osei at 64 is at the upper threshold. Casey Kim and Morgan Chen at 55 are within healthy range. Recommendation: cap any single interviewer at 65 panels per cycle; rebalance Sam Rivera immediately.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"panel_load_balancer\",\"category\":\"overload_risk\"},\"id\":\"panel-002\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Interviewer panel load distribution (10 interviewers, 200 candidates). Sam Rivera: 77 panels (highest load, 38.5% of candidates). Jamie Osei: 64 panels. Riley Patel: 61. Alex Wang: 61. Drew Singh: 61. Taylor Brooks: 59. Quinn Diaz: 57. Jordan Lee: 56. Morgan Chen: 55. Casey Kim: 55. Average load: 60.6 panels per interviewer. Standard deviation: ~6.5. Sam Rivera is a significant outlier at 27% above average — risk of burnout and inconsistent scoring.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"panel_load_balancer\",\"category\":\"interviewer_load_summary\"},\"id\":\"panel-001\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Interview scoring consistency and panel quality benchmarks. Average cumulative round score across all 200 candidates: calculated from data. Score variance increases with interviewer fatigue — overloaded interviewers show wider score ranges. Industry standard: inter-rater reliability (IRR) should be above 0.7 (Cohen's kappa) for technical roles. Best practice: run calibration sessions every 30 panels per interviewer. Panels with 3+ interviewers have better IRR than 1-2 person panels. Recommendation: require minimum 2 interviewers per panel for all senior and above roles.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"panel_load_balancer\",\"category\":\"scoring_consistency\"},\"id\":\"panel-005\"}"}]

evaluation.reasoning: The recommendation to rebalance panels is well-supported and matches the golden dataset's expectations for addressing overload.

insights.recommendation: Focus on reducing the offer decline rate by addressing decline reasons such as time-to-offer and competition, and aligning job descriptions with actual role scopes.

evidence.key_insight: The current decline rate of 39.7% is significantly higher than the industry benchmark acceptance rate of 70-80%, indicating a need for actions to improve acceptance.

alternative.trade_off: Lower cost and faster processing, but potentially less nuanced insights into complex hiring dynamics.

(1) Address culture concerns: add team lunch/coffee with potential teammates before offer stage. (2) Reduce time-to-offer: 13% declined due to long timeline — target under 5 days from final interview to offer. (3) Competing offers: implement exploding offer policy with 72-hour decision window for senior roles. (4) Counter-offers: brief candidates on counter-offer likelihood during offer call. (5) Role scope: align JD with actual role scope — 3 declines suggest role was mis-scoped. (6) Compensation: ensure offer is within 5% of candidate's stated expectation gathered at screening.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"offer_insights\",\"category\":\"offer_improvement_actions\"},\"id\":\"offer-003\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Offer acceptance rates by sourcing channel. insights.recommendation: Escalate communication with hiring managers for roles exceeding SLA, especially in the Senior and Lead categories.

evidence.key_insight: The current average days in pipeline indicates overall compliance, but the SLA breaches highlight significant delays, especially in Senior and Lead roles.

alternative.trade_off: Lower accuracy in pipeline health insights.

insights.rag_context: [{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Overall pipeline funnel summary (200 candidates). Applications: 200. Reached offer stage: 57 (28.5%). Accepted offers: 34 (17.0%). Rejected: 114 (57.0%). Still active/not yet decided: 29 (14.5%). Position type breakdown: Engineering 102 (51%), Data Science 41 (20.5%), Product 38 (19%), Design 19 (9.5%). Position level: Senior 81 (40.5%), Staff 56 (28%), Lead 25 (12.5%), Mid 25 (12.5%), Principal 13 (6.5%). Pipeline is heavily skewed toward Senior and Staff levels — calibrate SLA targets accordingly.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"pipeline_health\",\"category\":\"pipeline_funnel_summary\"},\"id\":\"pipe-001\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Time-to-hire SLA benchmarks by position level. Mid-level roles: SLA target 21-28 days from application to offer. Senior roles: SLA target 28-35 days. Lead roles: SLA target 35-42 days. Staff roles: SLA target 35-45 days. Principal roles: SLA target 42-55 days. Average rounds by level in this dataset: Lead 3.32, Senior 3.01, Mid 2.84, Principal 2.54, Staff 2.39. Staff has fewer rounds but longer calendar time due to scheduling complexity. Every reschedule adds 3-5 days; 50% of candidates had 2+ reschedules — expect pipeline to run long.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"pipeline_health\",\"category\":\"sla_benchmarks_by_level\"},\"id\":\"pipe-002\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Sourcing effectiveness by position type. Engineering roles (102 of 200 candidates, 51%) are sourced across all channels. Data Science (41 candidates, 20.5%) responds best to Hackathon and Referral. Product (38 candidates, 19%) sourced primarily via Company Website and LinkedIn. Design (19 candidates, 9.5%) — smallest pool — sourced mostly via Referral and Glassdoor. Recommendation: tailor channel strategy per role type rather than using a single mix.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"sourcing_quality\",\"category\":\"role_type_sourcing\"},\"id\":\"src-005\"}"},{"type":"text","text":"{\"pageContent\":\"Pipeline velocity benchmarks for technical hiring. Industry average time-to-hire by role: Software Engineer 35-45 days, Senior Engineer 42-52 days, Staff/Principal 55-70 days, Data Scientist 40-55 days. Interview-to-offer conversion benchmark: 35-45% (current dataset: 28.5% — below benchmark). Offer-to-acceptance conversion benchmark: 72-78% (current: 59.6% — below benchmark). Sourcing-to-screen conversion: typically 20-30% of applicants reach phone screen. Pipeline health score formula: (Offer rate × Acceptance rate) / SLA compliance × 100.\",\"metadata\":{\"agent\":\"pipeline_health\",\"category\":\"velocity_metrics\"},\"id\":\"pipe-004\"}"}]

evaluation.reasoning: The recommendation to escalate communication for SLA breaches is well-grounded and aligns with the golden dataset's expectations.
